sentences.text: There were roll-ups.
They were asking all the section, a few streets, to go out to a big place, like was, like a big market.
And over there they started to segregate people, left and right.
Nobody knew what was left.
Nobody knew what was right.
Anyways, my mother was sent away that time with the transport like I mentioned to an unknown destination. 'Til today, we don't know exactly where she went.
But what we heard, they sent her by transport in the cattle wagons.
And many of them suffocated in the cart.
And finally, when they brought them to destination, they brought them to one of the extermination places.
And she was gassed, what I heard, and burned.
When we came back home, it was a dramatic situation because we cared for each other so much.
But my father was a very strong man, like I mentioned before.
He was a weight-lifter, a fantastically built man.
He was 41 years old.
And he took it in a terrible, terrible way.
We all were crying.
But this man couldn't cry.
But we knew what was happening to him.
And he was taking us around, including my little sister, who was that time six, seven years old.
And he said look, what happened is a tragic situation, and I cannot live that way anymore.
Once I was walking a few days later, the streets with my brother, and I saw standing against a building on a brick wall, somebody was crying terribly.
We walked to quiet this man down, but we see it was our father.
So when we took him around, we started to cry.
He said he cannot take anymore.
He has to avenge his wife's death.
We had a meeting at that time.
It was a quite well-known story in the city of Krakow.
By then we were sent out to the airport, my brother and myself, by the end of 1942.
They were building barracks for a few hundred workers at the airport.
And my father said to us, look, I hope you'll be able to survive.
I had an aunt and an uncle.
They were childless.
They loved my little sister very much.
So my father said the auntie would take care of her.
You go over there because you have a better chance to survive.
But we never knew, as he joined, already, the underground in the city of Krakow.
He disappeared from ghetto in 1942.
I never saw my father again.
Like I said before, he was a very strong man.
I never met such a strong man in my lifetime.
He could do anything.
Naturally, as a young man, in your father you see always the giant of the man, but he was.
And what he did, he did because he couldn't live by himself, not to avenge his wife.
What I heard, he was in the city preparing all kinds of bombs underground.
They were throwing on different section.
One of them is a famous place, they called it the Nightclub [?
Sigonaria, ?] where the Germans used to come to enjoy themselves.
He was one of the men who would throw the bomb on the [?
Sigonaria ?] Nightclub.
And many, many other things, what people were telling me because I didn't know anything about it.
What I find out later on, in the beginning, in February, in 1943, he was arrested, was thrown into a jail of Gestapo.
They used to call it Montelupi-- was a terrible, terrible jail.
And they were trying to find out from him many, many things about his activities during the time when he left ghetto.
People were telling me that he was tortured for weeks.
Finally, practically, all [INAUDIBLE] they brought to Plaszow, to the concentration camp.
Actually, it wasn't a concentration camp.
It was a labor camp, Plaszow, where the famous killer, this bloodthirsty man, Amon Goeth, was the commander of the camp.
And he was killed on the famous, they were calling Hujowa Gorka, it was on a mountainside.
He was shot, and his body was burned with a number of other people.
Their name was Friana, F-R-I-A-N-A Diamond.
Yes, a few times.
But didn't last long because in 1943 was the tragic, another tragic day in our life.
They were liquidating the ghetto, and my aunt had the chance to go to the Camp Plaszow.
But she refused.
Her husband was begging, or crying and begging her to go with him because they didn't want to let my little sister.
She said, no.
She refused.
Her husband was separated from her.
He went to the Camp Plaszow.
And in 19-- 13 of March, 1943, when they were liquidating the ghetto, my sister and my aunt got killed in the ghetto by the German.
How did they find out?
My uncle, a day or a few days later, was sent up to the Plaszow, to the ghetto, in order to clean up the mess what the German did with the liquidation of the ghetto.
When he was cleaning up, he found my little sister and his wife together in her arms.
Both were killed.
The man broke down terribly.
By the way, he didn't go through the Holocaust.
He died, I don't remember.
I don't know how.
Yes, we knew that day because we got news, the liquidation of the ghetto, the 13 of March, 1943.
At the airport?
Wasn't bad.
Because we weren't surrounded by SS or the Gestapo people.
We were surrounded by the Wehrmacht.
And their attitude wasn't that vicious like the SS or SD or the Gestapo because they were more human.
Naturally, it wasn't any life for a Jew.
But still, the treatment was much better than anytime by the SS-controlled guard.
At the airport, yes.
We had enough food.
At the airport, I was working in a garage, and I was a helper to auto body repairs.
It was auto bodies or trucks or any heavy machinery.
And I was working in a very, very big garage under the control of the Wehrmacht.
Many German soldier was working us, the meisters, like the foremans, and what have you.
They weren't too sympathetic either to our cause because each time I didn't bring fast enough a tool what they asked or anything like that, I got kicked or beat up.
This didn't stop for a moment.
What I remember during that time, there were another three men working with me.
It's well-known, their name were Backalash.
They were from outside of Krakow.
Those three men ran away from the airport.
They got connection.
I heard they were quite rich.
They had money in different places.
They ran away to Hungary.
Some channels took them away.
When I find out about it, I didn't know anything about it.
But the German came in, and they were asking me, how come you didn't notify our officer if you knew something like that happen?
I said, I never knew it about their escape or planning or anything because they never spoke to me.
Anyways, didn't help me.
They beat me up, and I had to strip myself.
And I got on my behind 10 very badly-- with a big strap.
I couldn't sit for a number of weeks.
I had to stay up or lying on my front.
All three men survived the war in Hungary.
As of today, one man is alive.
He lives in South America.
His name is Benno.
It's the youngest one.
He's still alive.
What I heard-- I never met him after the war.
They came to us.
They said, it's coming from Plaszow, and we have to return to the camp because they're liquidating the barracks for all the Jews who are working on the airport.
This happened, if I'm not mistaken, around August, September, 1943.
We were packed on trucks, and they took us to Plaszow.
When we came to Plaszow, we saw the tremendous difference.
What is happening, like, I met many of my friends what I couldn't recognize them.
They lost so much weight because there wasn't enough food.
They didn't have anything to wear.
It was miserable.
And people were working doing 12 and 14 hours every day.
And nobody was trying to help them because they were, all of them, under certain scare.
Each time they were calling on this Appellplatz again, a call roll, or a roll call, and they were segregating, each and every time, a number of people.
Each time after a call like that, a few thousand people were sent away to an unknown destination.
Many got burnt immediately.
Some of them survived, but not too many.
This was going on during the whole year in Plaszow.
And practically every few weeks, people were coming from different camps.
They were thrown in.
And the average was always between 20,000 and 25,000 of prisoners in the camp of Plaszow.
Yes.
When I arrived, there were a number of selections like I mentioned.
Every few weeks was another selection.
They were calling hundreds, thousands, sometimes the whole camp had to-- sometimes they segregate by barracks.
In each and every barrack was approximately 300 to 400 prisoners were [? scrap ?] on three levels on bunks.
In order to get in, you had to slide in, because the ceiling between them was only 24 inches.
So was a very, very bad situation in Plaszow from the beginning of time.
People didn't have enough food, enough clothing.

And the segregation what was taking care, people got terribly scared, because each and every time we're called outside-- and this could happen during wintertime, where [COUGH] could be 10, 15, 20 below.
We're not properly dressed.
Many died, just standing.
Many they took away.
And most of the time, the segregation took away 2,000 to 3,000 people each time.
Where they were sending, we had the wagon, the cattle wagon in the camp.

And they were sending away, practically every few weeks, 2,000 to 3,000, because each and every time, more people were arriving from different camps what they were liquidating around the city of Krakow.
But the average, like I mentioned, was around 20,000 people to 25,000 all the time.
Yes.
My brother, Roman, luckily, he was always with me.
And I am very thankful to this man because his approach was much more optimistic than my attitude.
I never believed for a moment I could survive.
Never.
He was building me up all the time.
He said, we will survive.
And we will survive.
We have to avenge our parents, our people, our family.
And he was the strong man behind me always.
Yes.
I was sent out to a barrack.
They call it the metal shop.
And I was doing all kinds of works, whatever they ask me to do it.
They had all kinds of machine.
And each and every prisoner had to do a certain process on certain machines.
And I was working on one of-- on one machine like that too.
No.
When I entered Plaszow, my contact with the outside completely disappeared because was impossible already to get through anybody.
Any move was controlled by so many people.
It was a terrible, scary situation in the camp because we had to work sometimes outside.
And different-- they need people.
The German could walk in to our barracks, take 20, 30 boys.
They were all young men between 18 and 20, 22.
And we had to do all kinds of work.
And I remember, like today, we're always trying to avoid, if we could, to go on the outside.
Because Amon Goeth, the commander from the camp, he was a vicious man, and all his people surrounded him, to keep for them was-- when they saw blood, it was like a shark.
When they see blood, they're getting vicious.
This was happening with every single German, every single assessment.
When they saw blood, the killing started.
They were never satisfied with one, two, or three or more killings.
They could kill as many possible.
The worst of them was the commandant, Amon Goeth, who was approximately around 6 feet 6 inches tall, around 275-pounder.
He was vicious.
When he was riding his horse, he was riding a very big white horse.
I remember like today.
And his sport was-- because this was on a mountainside-- and once, over there, was a cemetery.
The whole mountain was a cemetery.
They demolished the cemetery, and with the tombstones, they paved streets to go to his villa, to Amon Goeth.
He was a blood-thirsty killer.
He had two dogs, Great Danes.
They were killers.
He was training them on his command.
They jump anybody, and in no time, they kill him.
They had that grab on the throat, and they got killed in minutes.
Many, many prisoners got killed that way.
On top of it, this man was riding his horse, each time he saw somebody single and didn't do something that same minute, he killed him.
There were a number of situation where they brought, let's say 50 people from outside to work.
One of them brought a small chicken.
He got it, and he was trying to bring into the camp.
Anyways, when they were checking the 50 people, they found this chicken hidden behind the young boy.
They called Amon Goeth, who was the commandant.
He came down from his villa, and he asked, who had the chicken because-- the boy came out, and he said, I got it.
He killed him instantly.
He walked around--
With a gun.
And he was walking behind each and every one.
And every second person got killed the same way.
When he didn't have enough ammunition, he asked another SS man to give him the gun and was another repetition of killing.
That time I heard he killed 50, 25 people.
But there were many incidents like that.
And this way, many, many people got their life-- lost their lives.
No.
It was impossible to escape from this camp because we had towers that support the electric wires.
And we had towers every 150 to 200 feet with machine guns and reflectors.
At night was like daytime.
Nobody could escape.
Was impossible.
No.
Was impossible.
No.
No.
We were left completely alone, like we knew, that time to die.
Survival is a very strong instinct.
This is the strongest instinct what the person possess.
To survive means to do anything in order to stay alive.
Was extremely hard because it wasn't up to us.
It was up to another strong power.
With each and every German had the power to destroy me or anybody else if he wishes to.
But the instinct of survival, this what kept me going all the time.
In Plaszow, I was approximately one year, til the middle of October, 1944, where a few days before, somebody mentioned to us we are on Schindler's list.
Yes, very much so.
Because we knew whoever was working for Schindler in his factory had a much better life.
The man was more sympathetic.
Naturally, there were a number of phases during the war which we have to understand because that time when Schindler-- can I say anything about Schindler?
Because when Schindler came to Krakow, and he took over the factory-- it happened in 1939 in the month of December-- he was introduced to a few people.
Between them was Abraham Bankier who was the owner of the enamel factory.
Naturally, when he came in, he became the director, as a German, and a card holder because, whatever it is, Oskar Schindler was an opportunist.
He came.
He was a failure most of his life.
But from 1939, when he came, he became a director in that enamel factory.
They call it Zablocie, this part of the city.
His life changed tremendously.
So he got involved.
He was smart.
And he got involved with this owner, what was the name, Abraham Bankier, and Itzhak Stern.
Itzhak Stern is portrayed, as you know, by the-- the actor who was playing his role.
What is his-- yeah-- And from that day on, he was taking care more and more and more into the factory.
One day-- one day, he got introduced to another three, four people, and they were bringing money in order to get into this factory.
Many of them with money got into this factory.
And, by the way, the role of Itzhak Stern is played by Ben Kingsley in the movie.
What I heard, they were trying to bribe Schindler that time in order to work for him.
How much is true, I don't know.
But that's what I heard through those years.
But most of the time, Schindler got a few hundred people from the ghetto.
And when they liquidated the ghetto, they were coming from Plaszow, from the camp.
So he was trying to get-- to be very friendly with Amon Goeth, who was the commandant of Plaszow, in order to get the labor.
